@graph.name: BMW models fitted with the M20

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M20 engine replacement cost?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M20 typically costs £1,500–£2,200 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], varying by displacement and condition. Fitting adds £650–£1,200 depending on labour rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M20 a reliable engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes – the M20 is one of BMW's most durable engines. Its cast-iron block and simple SOHC design give it excellent longevity. The Achilles' heel is the timing belt – if changed on schedule, these engines easily exceed 200,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What is the most common BMW M20 failure?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing belt failure is the most catastrophic – if the belt breaks, valves bend, often writing off the engine. This is entirely preventable with routine replacement. Also common are oil leaks and cooling system deterioration.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW models use the M20?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M20 powered the E21 320/6 and 323i, E30 320i/323i/325i, E28 520i/525e/525i, early E34 520i/525i, and E24 628CSi/633CSi/635CSi.

mainEntity.name: How long does the BMW M20 last?

acceptedAnswer.text: With proper maintenance – especially regular timing belt changes – the M20 is known to exceed 200,000 miles. Many examples have reached 300,000+ miles on original bottom ends. Overheating and belt neglect are the main killers.

hero.h1: BMW M20 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The BMW M20 is a durable, single‑overhead‑cam straight‑six petrol engine — loved for its simplicity and torque, but timing belt changes are critical. Backed by real UK specialist enquiries [ESTIMATED].

verdictRating.confidence: 88% confidence — based on specialist workshop consensus and extensive owner‑club documented history.

rows.score: 16 (Simple engine; timing belt replacement is routine; parts affordable and widely available)
16 (Parts are cheap and available from multiple UK suppliers same‑day (new and used))
12 (Reconditioned cost vs. lowest host‑value (~£2,500) = ~72% – near 75% threshold → 12)

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: The M20 is a robust, torquey straight‑six — but the timing belt must be changed every 50,000 miles — buy one with documented belt history.
verdictRating.bestFor: Enthusiasts wanting a classic, simple, and characterful BMW six‑cylinder.
verdictRating.avoidIf: You're not prepared for routine timing‑belt and cooling‑system maintenance.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M20 typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [BMW‑QUOTE].

items.question: How much does a BMW M20 engine replacement cost?
items.answer: A reconditioned M20 typically costs £1,500–£2,200 supply only [BMW‑QUOTE], varying by displacement and condition. Fitting adds £650–£1,200 depending on labour rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

items.question: Is the BMW M20 a reliable engine?
items.answer: Yes – the M20 is one of BMW's most durable engines. Its cast‑iron block and simple SOHC design give it excellent longevity. The Achilles' heel is the timing belt – if changed on schedule (every 50,000 miles or 4‑5 years), these engines easily exceed 200,000 miles.

items.question: What is the most common BMW M20 failure?
items.answer: Timing belt failure is the most catastrophic – if the belt breaks, valves bend, often writing off the engine. This is entirely preventable with routine replacement. Also common are oil leaks (rocker cover gasket, rear main seal) and cooling system deterioration (water pump, radiator).

items.question: Which BMW models use the M20?
items.answer: The M20 powered the E21 320/6 and 323i, E30 320i/323i/325i, E28 520i/525e/525i, early E34 520i/525i, and E24 628CSi/633CSi/635CSi.

items.question: How long does the BMW M20 last?
items.answer: With proper maintenance – especially regular timing belt changes – the M20 is known to exceed 200,000 miles. Many examples have reached 300,000+ miles on original bottom ends. Overheating and belt neglect are the main killers.
